The Geometric Distribution
Binomial mean equals np; standard deviation equals √(np(1−p)).
Geometric variable counts trials until first success in independent binary trials.
Sample statistics vary; this variation can be random or systematic.
Continuous random variables take any value; intervals have associated probabilities.
Each confidence interval either contains or doesn't contain the true population proportion.
Use one-sample z-interval for population proportion confidence intervals.
Sampling distribution shows all possible statistic values from samples of fixed size.
Distribution shape variation can result from random or systematic causes.
Unbiased estimators equal the population parameter on average.
Sample proportion distribution has mean p and standard deviation √(p(1−p)/n).
Difference in sample means has mean μ1 − μ2 and specified standard deviation formula.
Difference in sample proportions has mean p1-p2 and specified standard deviation formula.
Sample mean distribution has same mean as population, standard deviation reduced by √n.
Numbers gain meaning through context, conveying information about variation.
Variables are characteristics that differ across individuals in data.
Frequency tables show counts; relative frequency tables show proportions.
Bar charts display frequencies or proportions for categorical variables.
Null distribution of regression slope follows t-distribution under conditions.
Discrete variables take countable values, finite or countably infinite.
Use t-test to test the slope of a regression model.
Confidence intervals for slope capture true population parameter approximately C% of times.
Quantitative distributions are described by shape, center, variability, and unusual features.
Residuals estimate deviations; sample SD estimates population SD with n−2.
Scatter plot variation around a line can be random or systematic.
Statistics are numerical summaries computed from sample data.
Chi-square test statistic formula uses observed and expected counts with df calculation.
Null hypothesis: no difference in categorical distributions across populations; alternative: difference exists.
Calculate cell expected counts using row total times column total divided by table total.
Chi-square test statistic formula uses observed and expected counts with category-based degrees of freedom.
Expected counts equal sample size multiplied by null hypothesis probability.
Five-number summary comprises minimum, Q1, median, Q3, maximum.
Observed versus expected variation can be random or statistically significant.
Use graphs like histograms and boxplots to compare multiple data distributions.
Data patterns and associations can occur randomly or represent real relationships.
Multiple graph types display one categorical variable subdivided by another.
Marginal relative frequencies equal row/column totals divided by table total.
Correlation r measures strength and direction of linear association between variables.
Two-sample t-test statistic follows t-distribution with df between min(n₁−1, n₂−1) and n₁+n₂−2.
Linear regression uses explanatory variable to predict response variable.
Least-squares regression minimizes squared residuals and passes through mean point.
Use two-sample t-test to compare means of two quantitative populations.
Confidence level indicates proportion of intervals capturing true difference.
Outliers have large residuals and deviate from the general trend.
Sampling distribution of difference of means is normal under specified conditions.
Population is the complete set of all subjects of interest.
Test statistic t follows t-distribution with n−1 degrees of freedom.
Use one-sample t-test when population standard deviation is unknown.
Sampling without replacement selects items once; with replacement allows repeated selection.
Each confidence interval either contains or doesn't contain the true population mean.
t-distribution has heavier tails than normal distribution when using sample standard deviation.
Random variation causes errors and uncertainty in statistical inference conclusions.
Bias systematically favors certain responses over others in sampling.
Test statistic for two proportions uses pooled proportion and combines standard errors.
Type I error: rejecting a true null hypothesis; false positive result.
Different experimental designs have tradeoffs based on research context and constraints.
Statistical conclusions drawn from data reflect the underlying population distribution.
Null hypothesis for difference of proportions specifies zero difference.
Data patterns can occur even when variation is fundamentally random.
Random processes produce outcomes determined by chance, not certainty.
Repeated sampling produces confidence intervals capturing true difference C% of time.
Sample space contains all possible non-overlapping outcomes of random process.
Use two-sample z-interval for comparing two population proportions.
Joint probability measures the likelihood of both events occurring simultaneously.
Conditional probability measures likelihood of event A given event B occurred.
Independent events: occurrence of one doesn't affect the other's probability.
Significance level α is predetermined probability of Type I error.
Random variables represent numerical results from random processes or trials.
Null distribution can be randomization or theoretical (z) distribution.
Parameters are fixed numerical values characterizing populations or distributions.
Linear combination mean equals linear combination of means.
Null hypothesis assumes truth; alternative hypothesis is what we test for.
Construct probability distributions via rules or random simulation methods.
Sample is a subset selected from the population.
Without replacement sampling produces smaller standard deviation; negligible when n < 10% of population.
Mean of discrete random variable equals sum of values times probabilities.
Matched pairs become one sample; find differences, then test like population mean.
Compare p-value to α; reject H₀ if p-value ≤ α.
C% confidence means the interval likely captures the true population mean.
Normal distributions model populations using bell-shaped curves.
SRS gives every group equal selection chance using random number generators or tables.
Sampling without replacement reduces standard deviation; negligible if samples are less than 10% of populations.
Mutually exclusive events cannot happen together; their intersection probability is zero.
Increasing degrees of freedom makes t-distribution approach normal distribution shape.
Null has equality; alternative has strict inequality. One-sided or two-sided based on question.
Slope sampling distribution is unbiased with SD dependent on sigma and x spread.
Multiplication rule: joint probability equals P(A) times conditional probability P(B|A).
Binomial variable counts successes across n independent two-outcome trials.
P-value determined using chi-square distribution table or computational technology.
Volunteer samples are typically unrepresentative due to voluntary response bias.
Mean equals sum of all values divided by total count of values.
Outliers are unusually small or large data points relative to others.
Without replacement sampling reduces standard deviation; negligible if n < 10% of population.
Null hypothesis: no association between categorical variables; alternative: association exists.
P-value assumes null hypothesis true: both population proportions are equal.
Independent events have conditional probabilities equal to marginals and multiplicative intersection.
Null distribution modeled by randomization or theoretical chi-square distribution under null hypothesis.
Type II error: failing to reject false null hypothesis; false negative result.
Interpret slope confidence intervals with sample context and population details.
Chi-square statistic quantifies discrepancy between observed and expected counts.
Null hypothesis states equal proportions or zero difference.
Independence means one variable's information doesn't affect the other's distribution.
Boxplot displays five-number summary with box, median line, whiskers, outliers.
Null hypothesis states slope equals hypothesized value; alternative is one or two-sided.
Confidence level expresses how confident we are interval captures true proportion.
Random assignment enables detection of treatment effects unlikely due to chance alone.
Use numerical summaries like mean and standard deviation to compare datasets.
Estimators show variability that probability distributions can model.
Discrete random variables have countable values with probabilities summing to one.

Continuous variables take uncountably infinite values with infinite divisibility.
P-value interpretation assumes null hypothesis true: population means equal.
Check independence and appropriate sampling distribution for inference validity.
Conditional relative frequency represents one part relative to its row or column total.

Z-statistic formula: (sample statistic minus null value) divided by standard deviation.
Correlation coefficient calculated via formula or technology, typically technology used.
Outcomes are individual results from single trials of random processes.
Null assumes equal means; alternative specifies one-tailed or two-tailed differences.

Predicted values calculated using ŷ = a + bx equation with intercept and slope.
Bar height represents count or proportion within each category.
Interpretations must reference sample characteristics and represented population details.
Slope equals correlation times ratio of standard deviations of variables.
Percentages, relative frequencies, and rates convey equivalent proportional information.
Geometric probability function: P(X = x) = (1 − p)^(x−1) p for trial x.
Interpretation must reference samples and describe populations represented.

Use two-sample t-interval for confidence intervals of difference between means.

High-leverage points have extreme x-values compared to other observations.
Categorical variables represent group membership or category names, not numbers.
Probability equals favorable outcomes divided by total outcomes when equally likely.

Large sample means follow approximately normal distributions regardless of population shape.

Quantitative variables represent numerical measurements or counts of quantities.
Categorical counts and frequencies support contextual data claims and interpretations.

Strong correlation alone does not guarantee linear model appropriateness or validity.
Summary statistics support data claims in context.

Extrapolation beyond data range produces less reliable predictions.
Y-intercept may lack meaningful interpretation in real-world context.
Influential points substantially change slope, intercept, or correlation when removed.

Events group multiple outcomes together from random processes.

Calculate exact binomial probabilities using the binomial probability function formula.

Area under normal curve equals probability for interval.
CLT requires independent observations and sufficiently large sample size conditions.
Sample statistics serve as point estimators for population parameters.

Difference in proportions is approximately normal when all four conditions exceed ten.
Normal population distribution yields normal sampling distribution of sample means.
Normal populations yield normal sampling distribution for difference in sample means.
Verify random sampling, 10% rule, and np̂ ≥ 10 and n(1−p̂) ≥ 10 conditions.
Repeated sampling produces intervals; approximately C% capture true population proportion.
Population proportion null hypothesis has form p equals p-naught value.

Rejection supports alternative hypothesis; non-rejection does not.
Significance level α equals probability of Type I error under null hypothesis.
Calculate confidence interval using point estimate and standard error formula.
Confidence intervals provide evidence supporting contextual claims about proportion differences.
Alternative hypotheses can be one-sided or two-sided directional statements.
Compare p-value to alpha to decide rejecting or failing to reject null hypothesis.
Use one-sample t-interval when population standard deviation is unknown for quantitative data.
Interpretation must reference the sample and the population it represents.
Null is μ = μ₀; alternative is one-sided or two-sided based on context.
Compare p-value to α to decide whether to reject null hypothesis.
